title: Appendix A — Data Documentation: NSFW
text:
A.1 Report Question
Are office workers at greater risk of injury (particularly falls) than non-office/manufacturing workers, and how does injury severity compare?

title: Appendix A — Data Documentation: NSFW
section: A.2 Data Source 1: BLS Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII)
text:
A.2 Data Source 1: BLS Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII)
(Bureau of Labor Statistics n.d.b)
Who collected it: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), with state agency partners.
Why it was collected: Required by the OSH Act of 1970 to track nonfatal workplace injuries and illnesses nationwide.
What it contains: Annual nonfatal injury and illness counts and rates by industry, occupation, and event type (e.g., falls, slips, trips). Key measures include Total Recordable Case (TRC) rates, Days Away From Work (DAFW) rates, and median days away from work. (Bureau of Labor Statistics 2026b)
When: Published annually. Most recent release covers 2023–2024 (published January 22, 2026).
Where: U.S. private industry and state/local government establishments. Excludes federal workers and the self-employed.
How: A stratified random sample of 200,000 employers report injuries from their OSHA 300 Log. BLS calculates incidence rates per 100 full-time equivalent (FTE) workers.
Structure: Published as XLSX and HTML tables. The dta shows injury rates by NAICS industry code, SOC occupation code, and event/exposure type. Suppressed cells are shown as a dash (—).
Quality control: BLS calculates relative standard errors for all estimates and flags unreliable cells. (Known limitation: employer self-reporting may undercount injuries).
License: Public domain (U.S. federal government). Free to use and download.
Relevance to report: Tables R4/R8 (industry × event type) and R9/R100 (occupation × event type) allow direct comparison of fall injury rates between office-sector and manufacturing industries. Median DAFW measures injury severity.

title: Appendix A — Data Documentation: NSFW
section: A.3 Data Source 2: BLS Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI)
text:
A.3 Data Source 2: BLS Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI)
(Bureau of Labor Statistics n.d.a)
Who collected it: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), with state agencies and federal partners.
Why it was collected: To provide a complete count of all fatal work injuries in the U.S. each year, covering the most severe end of the injury spectrum.
What it contains: Fatal injury counts and rates by industry, occupation, and event type (including falls). Fatal rates are expressed per 100,000 FTE workers. (Bureau of Labor Statistics 2026a)
When: Published annually. Most recent release covers 2024 (published February 19, 2026).
Where: All 50 U.S. states and D.C. Covers all workers including self-employed.
How: A census, Every fatal work injury is identified and verified using at least two independent source documents from the following: death certificates, OSHA reports, workers’ compensation records, news reports, etc..
Structure: Published as XLSX and HTML tables. The data shows fatal counts and rates by NAICS industry, SOC occupation, and event/exposure type. Suppressed values shown as a dash (—).
Quality control: Each case is independently verified through multiple source documents.
License: Public domain (U.S. federal government). Free to use and download.
Relevance to report: Table 2 breaks down fatal injuries by industry sector and event type (falls, slips, trips), enabling comparison of both severity and risk between office and manufacturing sectors.

title: Appendix A — Data Documentation: NSFW
text:
A.5 Conclusion
The SOII covers the probability and severity of nonfatal injuries by industry and event type, while the CFOI covers fatal injuries. Together they span the full injury severity spectrum. Both data sets include fall-specific breakdowns by industry and occupation which together directly address the safety officer’s concern about office worker fall risk compared to manufacturing workers.(Thought this data was intersiting to go through, and the overall concern was a bit comical)

title: 2  Summary

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
6.1 Conclusion
This report looked at the workplace injury risk across manufacturing and office sectors using both BLS nonfatal and fatal injury data. The findings confirm that manufacturing workers face much higher injury rates overall, but that falls are disproportionately common in office environments.
The nonfatal injury data showed that falls account for 33.6% of all injuries in finance and insurance, compared to just 21.6% in manufacturing. The fatal injury data showed the same pattern: falls caused 33.3% of deaths in finance and insurance versus 14.7% in manufacturing. T
These results suggest clear and practical next steps: safety officers in office settings should treat fall prevention as a top priority. Even though overall injury numbers are low, when office workers do get hurt, it is most likely from a fall. Simple changes such as slip-resistant flooring, clear walkways, proper lighting, and employee awareness programs can help to reduce this risk.
There are some limitations to note. The SOII relies on employer self-reporting, which may undercount injuries in workplaces with weaker safety cultures. Broad NAICS categories also include a wide range of job types, so rates may not perfectly represent desk-based workers specifically. Median days away from work (a useful measure of injury severity) was not available in the tables used here and would strengthen future analyses.
Overall, the data shows a clear case for workplace safety, recognizing fall risk as a significant concern even in low-injury office environments.

title: 4  Methods
text:
Injury rates for three sectors were extracted from the SOII Table R8 using NAICS codes: manufacturing (31–33), finance and insurance (52), and professional, scientific, and technical services (54). Finance and insurance and professional services were selected as the most representative office-type sectors.
For each sector, the following are recorded:

Total DAFW rate — overall nonfatal injury rate per 10,000 FTE workers
Fall-specific DAFW rate — nonfatal fall injuries per 10,000 FTE workers
Fall share of total injuries — falls as a percentage of all nonfatal injuries

Fatal injury counts were taken directly from CFOI Table A-1. Fall share of deaths are calculated by dividing fatal falls by total sector deaths.
No statistical tests were conducted. Both the SOII and CFOI represent population-level estimates, so the observed differences between sectors reflect real differences rather than sampling error.

title: Workplace Injury Risk: Office Workers vs. Manufacturing Workers
text:
0.1 Executive Summary
This report compares workplace injury risk between manufacturing workers and office workers (finance, insurance, and professional services) using 2023–2024 data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. The analysis covers both nonfatal injuries and workplace deaths.
Key findings:

title: Workplace Injury Risk: Office Workers vs. Manufacturing Workers
text:
Manufacturing workers are injured at a much higher rate overall — 86.4 injuries per 10,000 workers, compared to 11.0 in finance and insurance and 18.6 in professional services.
Falls make up a disproportionately large share of injuries in office sectors. Falls account for 33.6% of all injuries in finance and insurance, compared to 21.6% in manufacturing.
The same pattern holds for workplace deaths. Falls caused 33.3% of deaths in finance and insurance, versus 14.7% in manufacturing.
Recommendation: Office environments should prioritize fall prevention programs even though overall injury rates are low. Falls are the dominant injury risk in these settings.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1 Introduction
Most people assume that factories are far more dangerous than offices, and when it comes to overall injury rates, that assumption is correct. Manufacturing involves heavy machinery, physical labor, and industrial hazards that drive injury rates well above most other sectors.
But one specific hazard tells a more complicated story: falls. Slippery floors, cluttered walkways, and everyday movement in office settings can lead to falls that cause serious injuries. Understanding how fall risk compares across sectors matters for safety officers who need to allocate resources effectively.
This report addresses the question: Are office workers at greater risk of falls than manufacturing workers, and how do injuries compare in severity? To answer this, we use two federal datasets from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) that together cover the full spectrum of workplace injury, from minor incidents requiring days off work to fatal injuries.

title: 3  Data
text:
3.1.1 Nonfatal Injuries: SOII
The Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII) is published annually by the BLS, as required by the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 (Bureau of Labor Statistics n.d.b). It surveys approximately 200,000 employers and reports injury rates per 10,000 full-time equivalent (FTE) workers. The most recent release covers 2023–2024 (Bureau of Labor Statistics 2026b).
For this report, we used Table R8, which breaks down nonfatal injury rates by industry and event type (including falls, slips, and trips). We focused on Days Away From Work (DAFW) rates, which capture injuries serious enough to keep workers out of work.


3.1.2 Fatal Injuries: CFOI
The Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI) is an annual count of every workplace death in the United States (Bureau of Labor Statistics n.d.a). Unlike the SOII, which is a sample survey, the CFOI verifies each case using multiple independent sources such as death certificates, OSHA reports, and workers’ compensation records. The most recent release covers 2024 (Bureau of Labor Statistics 2026a).
We used Table A-1, which reports fatal injury counts by industry and event type, including falls.




Bureau of Labor Statistics. 2026a. “Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries Summary, 2024.” https://www.bls.gov/news.release/cfoi.nr0.htm.


———. 2026b. “Employer-Reported Workplace Injuries and Illnesses, 2023–2024.” https://www.bls.gov/news.release/osh.nr0.htm.


———. n.d.a. “Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI) — Current and Revised Data.” https://www.bls.gov/iif/fatal-injuries-tables.htm. Accessed February 21, 2026.


———. n.d.b. “Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses Data.” https://www.bls.gov/iif/nonfatal-injuries-and-illnesses-tables.htm. Accessed February 21, 2026.

title: 5  Results
text:
5.1.1 Nonfatal Injury Rates
As shown in Table 5.1, manufacturing workers experienced a total nonfatal injury rate of 86.4 per 10,000 FTE workers — roughly 8 times higher than the rate in finance and insurance (11) and 4.6 times higher than professional services (18.6).




Table 5.1: Nonfatal injury rates per 10,000 FTE workers by sector (SOII, 2023–2024)






Sector
Total Rate
Fall Rate
Falls as % of Total




Manufacturing
86.4
18.7
21.6%


Finance & Insurance
11.0
3.7
33.6%


Professional Services
18.6
4.3
23.1%










5.1.2 Fall-Specific Risk
While manufacturing workers have more falls in absolute terms, falls represent a much larger proportion of injuries in office sectors. As seen in Table 5.1, falls account for 33.6% of all nonfatal injuries in finance and insurance, and 23.1% in professional services — compared to just 21.6% in manufacturing.
This means that in office environments, falls are the dominant injury type. In manufacturing, falls are just one of many hazards competing for attention.


5.1.3 Fatal Injuries
Table 5.2 shows fatal injury counts and fall-related deaths by sector in 2024. Manufacturing had 353 workplace deaths, with 52 (14.7%) caused by falls. Finance and insurance had 18 total deaths, with 6 (33.3%) from falls. Professional services had 65 deaths, with 10 (15.4%) from falls.




Table 5.2: Fatal occupational injuries by sector (CFOI, 2024)

The pattern is consistent across both nonfatal and fatal data: falls are proportionally far more significant in office sectors than in manufacturing.


5.1.4 Summary of Key Comparisons
Table 5.3 provides a side-by-side comparison of the fall share of injuries across both nonfatal and fatal measures.
